claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask: Do you have a child (or children) you're claiming for?**

If yes, please tell me:
1. **How old is your child?** (or if you have multiple children, please tell me their ages)
2. **What are their names?** (This helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

The age of your child is important because:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children 20 or over typically don't qualify

What can you tell me about your child or children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 8 years old. He lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. So you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you.

Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check a few conditions about Alex:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, he almost certainly isn't working, so this should be a straightforward "yes" - but I need to confirm this to move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't work at all. He's only 8, so he's just in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that makes sense! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This means - is Alex receiving things like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

For an 8-year-old, this would be very unusual, so the answer is likely "yes" (meaning he's NOT receiving these benefits). Can you confirm?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in school, he's definitely not in an apprenticeship. This means the answer would be "not in apprenticeship" - can you confirm he's not in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through ANY of these ways:

1. **You normally live in the UK** - this is the most common route
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK?** This is usually the straightforward answer for most people.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you, which means you meet the responsibility requirement through the "living with the child" path.

You can be considered responsible if ANY of these apply:
1. **You live with the child** (which you already confirmed)
2. You contribute to the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents - at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since Alex lives with you, you definitely meet this requirement. Can you confirm that's still the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming for him

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. 

Is there anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex right now?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to understand Alex's living situation a bit more:

**Which of these describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (most common)
2. In hospital for an extended period
3. In care (looked after by the local council)
4. In a fostering arrangement

From what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I'm fostering Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that - that's important information! Let me check the fostering arrangement details.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. For fostering situations, there's a specific rule:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words:
- If the council is paying you fostering allowances or payments for Alex's accommodation or maintenance, you would NOT be eligible for Child Benefit
- If the council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, you CAN claim Child Benefit

Is the local council paying you for Alex's accommodation or maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council to foster Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You've already confirmed the council isn't paying you for Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Let me proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Even though you're fostering Alex, you can still claim Child Benefit because the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through a tax charge
5. **Payments** - Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:

You can find the application form and more details here: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit or the process?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under 16 and qualifies automatically; (2) He is not working (24+ hours per week); (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Although Alex is fostered, the local council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the foster carer can claim Child Benefit; (9) The council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance in any other way. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering Alex (age 8) and is eligible to claim Child Benefit because the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All other standard eligibility criteria have been satisfied including UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no other person claiming for the same child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

All eligibility criteria have been met.
